mnemonics.title: Process Control System Calls
mnemonics.content: FORK EXECutes WAITing for EXIT status - (fork, exec, wait, exit)

mnemonics.title: File Management Calls
mnemonics.content: Open Read Write Close LSeek - ORWCL (sounds like 'oruckle')

mnemonics.title: Six Categories Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: PFICDP - Process, File, Info, Communication, Device, Protection (alphabetical order)

mnemonics.title: Fork Return Values
mnemonics.content: Parent gets Child PID (positive), Child gets ZERO - Parent Positive, Child Zero

acronyms.usage: Refers to mechanisms like pipes, sockets, message queues that allow processes to communicate
Condition when read() returns 0, indicating no more data available
Unix permission model where file owners control access to their files
Unique number assigned to each process by the kernel for identification

visualTips.visualization: Think of each process having a personal array (starting at index 0,1,2 for stdin/stdout/stderr). Each slot points to an entry in the system-wide open file table, which then points to the actual file/inode.
fork() is like photocopying a document (creates identical copy). exec() is like replacing the entire document with a new one. Together: copy the template, then fill in new content.
A pipe is like a water pipe: one end for input (write), one end for output (read). Data flows in one direction only, and there's limited buffer capacity (typically 64KB in Linux).

analogies.analogy: Like entering a secure building: user mode is the lobby (public area), kernel mode is the vault (restricted). System call is like requesting entry to the vault - requires special permission and documentation.
A process is like a factory: has its own workspace (address space), equipment (CPU), inputs (files), and outputs. fork() builds a new identical factory, exec() converts it to make different products.

analogies.concept: File Descriptor as Restaurant Table Number
analogies.analogy: When you arrive at a restaurant (open a file), you get a table number (file descriptor). Other parties (processes) have their own table numbers. When you leave, the table is freed for others. stdin/stdout/stderr are like reserved VIP tables 0,1,2.
